request.question: Were both Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler authors?

title: Nancy Fairbanks
Elizabeth Chadwick
Beale family
Alan Chadwick
Connie May Fowler
Stephen F. Chadwick
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon
Before Women Had Wings
Fowler's position
Owen Chadwick
sentences: Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.
She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.
The Beales, together with the Fowlers, are a fictional family in the BBC soap opera "EastEnders".
They were the main family for storylines in early "EastEnders" and remained as such for many years.
Having appeared continuously from episode one, they are the show's longest serving family.
In 2004 Peter Beale and Lucy Beale were recast to give them a more mature look.
In 2007, there were few members of the Beale family left and no Fowlers, so the family were revamped.
Peter was recast again.
Ian Beale started a relationship with Jane Beale and Steven Beale also returned.
By 2010, the number of Beales had fallen again.
Lucy was recast and in 2013, Peter and Bobby were recast and Cindy Williams Jnr, the half-sister of Peter and Lucy, was reintroduced.
In addition to this at the start of 2014 Bex Fowler returned to the serial with her mother Sonia Fowler, with Martin Fowler following 11 months later in December.
In 2014 Lucy is found dead, causing her family and friends to struggle to come to terms with her death and the secrets she left behind, putting many people in the frame for her murder.
It was revealed on 19 February 2015 that she was killed by her own little brother, Bobby.
It was also revealed that Kathy Beale, who was presumed dead in 2006, was alive after having faked her death with her new husband in South Africa.
In December 2016 Michelle Fowler returned to Albert Square after originally leaving in 1995, although now played by Jenna Russell.
Members of the Beale family by blood currently in "EastEnders" include Ian, Michelle, Martin, Bex and Louie.
Additionally, several others may be considered Beales through marriage; Jane, Kathy, Sonia and Stacey Fowler are among these.
Also, Whitney Dean was adopted by Bianca Butcher, who is a member of the Beale family.
Lily Fowler and Arthur Fowler have both taken Martin's surname and have him as their stepfather.

Alan Chadwick (July 27, 1909 – May 25, 1980), English master gardener, was a leading innovator of organic farming techniques and influential educator in the field of biodynamic/French intensive gardening.
He was a student of Rudolf Steiner and is often cited as inspirational to the development of the "California Cuisine" movement.
The Chadwick restaurant in Beverly Hills was named after him.
His grave is marked by a stupa at the Green Gulch Farm Zen Center in California.

Chadwick is the subject of a 2013 retrospective by a former University of California, Santa Cruz professor, Paul Lee, called "There Is a Garden in the Mind: A Memoir of Alan Chadwick and the Organic Movement in California".
There is a memorial page at http://www.alan-chadwick.org/ that includes the 1971 16-mm film "Garden".
Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.
Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).
She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.
"Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.
Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.
Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".
"The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.
Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.
"How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.
Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.
It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.
Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).
Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.
Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon PC (10 December 1650 – 30 May 1701) was an English politician.
He was the son of Ferdinando Hastings, 6th Earl of Huntingdon, born in the 27th year of his parents' marriage, and became Earl of Huntingdon on 13 February 1656 on his father's death.
He married, firstly, Elizabeth Lewis, daughter of Sir John Lewis, 1st Baronet, on 19 February 1671/2.
She died 24th December 1688.
He married, secondly, Mary Frances Fowler, daughter of Francis Leveson Fowler, on 8 May 1690.
With his first wife he had two sons and six daughters, including Thomas (1674–1675), George and Elizabeth.
With his second wife he had two more sons and five more daughters, including Theophilus, Catherine, Maria and Margaret.
Before Women Had Wings is a 1997 television film, based on the story by Connie May Fowler about a mother whose abusive husband commits suicide.
She then starts to violently abuse her two daughters.
It is only then that a kindly black woman becomes good friends with her youngest daughter and helps the daughter escape her tortured life.
The film stars Ellen Barkin, Oprah Winfrey, Tina Majorino, and Julia Stiles.
Barkin won an Emmy for her role.
In medicine, Fowler's position is a standard patient position.
It is an intervention used to promote oxygenation via maximum chest expansion and is implemented during events of respiratory distress.
Fowler's position facilitates the relaxing of tension of the abdominal muscles, allowing for improved breathing.
In immobile patients and infants, the Fowler's position alleviates compression of the chest that occurs due to gravity.
Fowler's position increases comfort during eating and other activities, is used in postpartum women to improve uterine drainage, and in infants when signs of respiratory distress present.
The patient is placed in a semi-upright sitting position (45-60 degrees) and may have knees either bent or straight.
Fowler's position is also used when oral or nasal gastric feeding tubes have been implemented as it minimizes the risk of aspiration.
Peristalsis and swallowing are aided by the effect of gravitational pull.
William Owen Chadwick ( ; 20 May 1916 – 17 July 2015) was a British Anglican priest, academic, writer and prominent historian of Christianity.
As a leading academic, Chadwick became Dixie Professor of Ecclesiastical History from 1958 to 1968 and Regius Professor of History from 1968 to 1983.
From 1956 to 1983, Chadwick was elected and severed as the Master of Selwyn College, Cambridge.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Were both Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler authors?
hop1_docs: Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.

Stephen F. Chadwick | Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.  Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Lilian Fowler | Elizabeth Lilian Maud Fowler MBE, JP (7 June 1886 – 11 May 1954) was an Australian politician, and Australia's first female mayor.

Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.

Connie May Fowler | Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.  Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).  She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.  "Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.  Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.  Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".  "The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.  Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.  "How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.  Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.  It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.  Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).

Connie Needham | Connie Needham (born December 5, 1959) is an American actress and dance instructor.  As Connie Newton, she portrayed Elizabeth Bradford on the television series "Eight is Enough" from 1977 to 1981.
Conny Van Dyke | Conny Van Dyke (sometimes credited as Connie Van Dyke) is a singer and actress, born September 28, 1945 in Nassawadox, Virginia to Benjamin and Charlotte Elizabeth Van Dyke.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Elizabeth Chadwick: passage [1] states "Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions." (passage [1]). Passage [4] states "author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick" referring to Nancy Fairbanks Herndon (passage [4]).
  - Connie May Fowler: passage [5] states "Connie May Fowler ... is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet." (passage [5]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Two candidate references for "Elizabeth Chadwick" appear: a standalone "Elizabeth Chadwick" page (passage [1]) and a pseudonym usage on the "Nancy Fairbanks" / Nancy Herndon entry (passage [4]). Do not assume which is intended.
  - Connie May Fowler has a single matching passage (passage [5]) in the provided set.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity to resolve next hop: "Elizabeth Chadwick" (as named in the passages) and the related "Nancy Fairbanks" (Nancy Fairbanks Herndon) entry.
  - Precise missing fact needed: whether the Elizabeth Chadwick referred to is a distinct author page (and thus an author in her own right) or the pseudonym of Nancy Fairbanks Herndon; and confirmation on the Connie May Fowler page that she is an author (publication/roles) to satisfy "both".
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Elizabeth Chadwick" page — check the lede/infobox and 'Works'/'Bibliography' or 'Career' section for authorship, birth/year, and whether this is a distinct person. (Search: "Elizabeth Chadwick author lede works")
  2) Open the "Nancy Fairbanks" (or "Nancy Herndon") page — check lede and aliases/pseudonyms to confirm "author ... under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick." (Search: "Nancy Fairbanks Herndon pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick")
  3) Open the "Connie May Fowler" page — check lede/infobox and 'Works'/'Career' for novelist/essayist/memoirist credits and publication list. (Search: "Connie May Fowler lede works bibliography")
  4) If a disambiguation exists, fetch "Elizabeth Chadwick (disambiguation)" to resolve multiple individuals named Elizabeth Chadwick.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.

Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Marquess of Lede | The Marquess of Lede (Spanish: Marquesado de Lede) was a Flemish title in use during the Ancien Regime, Lede is a city in Flanders.

The Death Guard | The Death Guard is the only published novel of the English author Philip George Chadwick (1893 in Batley, Yorkshire – 1955 in Brighton, Sussex).  Although the author is virtually unknown to the wider public, his work has received attention from literary scholars.  The novel contains many themes later developed by L Ron Hubbard and James Blish.  Chadwick was a political thinker with socialist tendencies, a Fabian and subsequently an Independent and a disciple of H.G. Wells.

Chadwick Professor of Civil Engineering | The Chadwick Professor of Civil Engineering is a professorship at University College London.  It is named after Sir Edwin Chadwick, a Victorian social reformer who worked to improve sanitation conditions.  The professorship was established in 1898 as the Chadwick Professorship of Municipal Engineering, with Edwin Chadwick's son Osbert Chadwick serving as the first professor.

Lede Formation | The Lede Formation (Dutch: "Formatie van Lede" ; abbreviation: Ld) is a geologic formation in the subsurface of Belgium.  The formation is named after the town of Lede in East Flanders.  It consists of shallow-marine limestone and sandstone, deposited in the former sea that covered Belgium during the Eocene.

LEDE | The Linux Embedded Development Environment (LEDE) project is founded as a fork of the OpenWrt project and shares many of the same goals.  It was created in May 2016 by a group of core OpenWrt contributors due to disagreements on OpenWrt internal processes.  The schism was nominally reconciled a year later in May 2017 pending approval of the LEDE developers.  The proposed remerger will preserve the OpenWrt branding but use many of the LEDE processes and rules.  The remerge proposal vote was passed by LEDE developers in June 2017.
response.summary_2: Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions. Connie May Fowler is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.
